rulings.Case Title: JEAN W. SETO ET AL VS. KWOK HUNG SZETO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Charles Thompson To Appear For Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 1, PLAINTIFF JEAN SETO's Motion To Compel Charles Thompson To Appear For Deposition Pro Tem Judge Naomi Gray, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Having read and considered all timely filed papers and evidence, and good cause appearing therefor, it is tentatively ordered as follows. Absent written agreement of the parties, see Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2024.060, and absent order of the court, id. § 2024.050, lay "discovery proceedings" shall be "complete . . . on or before the 30th day . . . before the date initially set for the trial of the action," id. § 2024.020(a). Moreover, unless otherwise ordered, "a continuance or postponement of the trial date does not operate to reopen discovery proceedings." Id. § 2024.020(b). A trial court has discretion to hear a motion to compel after the cutoff date, but only upon "taking into consideration any matter relevant to the leave requested, including, but not limited to, the necessity and the reasons for the discovery and the diligence or lack of diligence of the party seeking the hearing of a discovery motion, and the reasons that the discovery motion was not heard earlier." Pelton-Shepherd Indus., Inc. v. Delta Packaging Prods, Inc., 165 Cal.App.4th 1568, 1588 (2008) (cleaned up). Since Plaintiff did not move to reopen discovery, Defendant has not had the opportunity to provide briefing on these issues. Accordingly, Plaintiff's motion is DENIED without prejudice to its renewal upon filing a motion to reopen discovery. As the prevailing parties, Defendants shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ngray@shadesofgray.law with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDRIY KRAVCHUK VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S Motion For Terminating And Monetary Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Naomi Gray, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Having read and considered all timely filed papers and evidence, and good cause appearing therefor, it is tentatively ordered as follows. Under the Discovery Act, "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code §§ 2030.290(c), 2031.300(c), 2025.480(j); see also Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2033.280. "Substantial justification as used in the discovery statutes means a justification that is well-grounded in both law and fact." Diepenbrock v. Brown, 208 Cal. App. 4th 743, 747 (2012) (cleaned up). "The burden of proving substantial justification for failing to comply with a discovery order compelling answers or production of documents and opposing a motion to compel compliance is on the losing party claiming that it acted with substantial justification." Doe v. U.S. Swimming, Inc., 200 Cal. App. 4th 1424, 1435 (2011) (cleaned up). Plaintiffs, representing themselves in pro per, aver that they sent the discovery "per Court's order." Defendants' reply states that Defendants have now received the discovery at issue. Accordingly, Defendants' motion is DENIED. Defendants' request for monetary sanctions is DENIED. All other sanctions are DENIED. As the prevailing parties, Defendants shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ngray@shadesofgray.law with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: WENDY HOUGH ET AL VS. TODD COURAGE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel; Documents From Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc.; Request For Sanctions Against Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc. In The Amount Of $892
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFFS WENDY HOUGH AND MATTHEW HRUDKA'S Motion To Compel; Documents From Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc.; Request For Sanctions Against Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc. In The Amount Of $892 Pro Tem Judge Naomi Gray, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Having read and considered all timely filed papers and evidence, and good cause appearing therefor, it is tentatively ordered as follows. "Any party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2017.010 (cleaned up). Plaintiffs have established the relevance of the discovery they seek. Plaintiffs' motion is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. Plaintiffs' request for documents is limited to the time period July 1, 2012 to the present. The custodian of records for Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc. may redact from the records the individual Defendants' personal payment source information, including bank account numbers, payment institution, credit card account information, or Social Security numbers, but may not redact the amount of any invoices or any third-party payment source information if the invoices were paid by persons or entities other than Defendants. Sophisticated Home Builder, Inc. shall provide written responses, responsive documents, and, if necessary, a privilege log within 20 calendar days of notice of entry of a final order on this motion. Plaintiffs' motion is otherwise DENIED. The request for sanctions is DENIED. As the prevailing parties, Plaintiffs shall prepare a form of the order recapitulating this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ngray@shadesofgray.law with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARIA JOZAMI VS. TWILIO INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Approval Of Paga Settlement And Dismissal Of Class Action Without Prejudice
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 6, PLAINTIFF MARIA JOZAMI'S Motion For Approval Of PAGA Settlement And Dismissal Of Class Action Without Prejudice. Plaintiff Maria Jozami's motion for approval of PAGA settlement and dismissal of class action without prejudice is granted. No opposition filed, and good cause shown. The Court finds that the proposed settlement is fair and adequate in view of the purposes and policies of PAGA, taking into account such factors as the adequacy of the civil penalties in light of the potential maximum if the matter were to go to verdict and the risks of litigation. (Lab. Code Sec. 2699(l)(2); s.See Flores v. Starwood Hotels & Resorts Worldwide, Inc. (C.D. Cal. 2017) 253 F.Supp.3d 1074, 1077; O'Connor v. Uber Technologies, Inc. (N.D. Cal. 2016) 201 F.Supp.3d 1110, 1132-1135.) In addition, the Court finds that the amount of attorney's fees and costs sought is reasonable. (Lab. Code Sec. 2699(g)(1).) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The Court will execute the proposed order and judgment included with the moving papers. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: RACHEL ARCHULETA VS. ARYAN KENCHIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs Sought By Defendant Enterprise Rent-A-Car Company Of San Francisco, Llc; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Declaration Of George M. Morris, Esq. In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 10, PLAINTIFF RACHEL ARCHULETA'S Motion To Tax Costs Sought By Defendant Enterprise Rent-A-Car Company Of San Francisco, LLC. Plaintiff Rachel Archuleta's motion to tax costs sought by Defendant Enterprise Rent-A-Car Company of San Francisco, LLC is denied. Plaintiff sued and served Enterprise as National Car Rental. On February 13, 2019, Defendants Enterprise and Enterprise Holdings, Inc. filed a demurrer to the complaint. On February 14, 2019, Plaintiff filed a request for dismissal without prejudice as to National Car Rental and Enterprise Holdings. As such, Enterprise (dba National) and Enterprise Holdings are prevailing parties entitled as a matter of right under Code of Civil Procedure section 1032(b) to recover costs. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 1032(a)(4) [prevailing party includes a defendant in whose favor a dismissal is entered]; Mon Chong Loong Trading Corp. v. Superior Court (2013) 218 Cal.App.4th 87, 94 ["While a lawsuit may be concluded by a voluntary dismissal, the price of such a dismissal is the payment of costs under section 1032," even if potential exists that plaintiff may refile the same action].) Plaintiff's reliance on cases involving statutory fee-shifting provisions is misplaced, as the definition of prevailing party for purposes of those provisions differs from that under the cost statute. (E.g., Salehi v. Surfside III Condominium Owners Ass'n (2011) 200 Cal.App.4th 1146, 1153.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to ngray@shadesofgray.law with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY S. BOSTWICK ET AL VS. SETERUS, INC., A BUSINESS ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 12, DEFENDANT SETERUS, INC.'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s motion to strike the claim for punitive damages is granted without leave to amend. First, plaintiff does not allege any facts showing "despicable" conduct. (See Mock v. Michigan Millers Mutual Ins. Co. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 306, 331.) Second, most cases that have addressed whether punitive damages are recoverable on a Rosenthal Act claim have ruled that they are not. (See Komarova v. National Credit Acceptance, Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 324, 341 n.3 ["It is not disputed that punitive damages are unavailable under the Act . . . Precluding causes of action for intentional infliction of emotional distress in Rosenthal Act cases thus serves only to eliminate recovery of punitive damages."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY S. BOSTWICK ET AL VS. SETERUS, INC., A BUSINESS ENTITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 12, Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint. Defendant Seterus, Inc.'s demurrer to the first amended complaint is overruled. In Davidson v. Seterus, Inc. (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 283, which Defendant failed to address in its moving papers, the court reversed the trial court's order sustaining the defendants' demurrer, holding squarely that "the Rosenthal Act's definition of 'debt collector' applies to a mortgage servicer who engages in debt collection practices in attempting to obtain repayment of mortgage debt." (Id. at 304; see also id. at 303 ["In our view, those federal courts that have concluded that section 1788.2 's definition of 'debt collector' may include mortgage lenders and mortgage servicers have the better position under an analysis of the actual statutory language."].) Defendant also argues, relying upon Obduskey v. McCarthy & Holthus LLP (2019) 139 S.Ct. 1029, that its December 17, 2018 letter to Plaintiffs (FAC Paras. 13, 15, 18) did not qualify as "debt collection." Obduskey does not bar Plaintiffs' claim. In Obduskey, the Supreme Court held only that an entity whose principal purpose is to enforce a security interest is not a "debt collector" under the federal Fair Debt Collection Practices Act. The Court explained that a law firm could not be liable for its conduct as part of the nonjudicial foreclosure process. In this case, Defendant's communications were not principally to enforce a security interest. Both the 12/12 and 12/17 letters were about making payments and "collecting" on the loan; the foreclosure process had yet to commence. Moreover, the definitions of "debt collector" under the Rosenthal Act and the FDCPA are different. (See Davidson v. Seterus, 21 Cal.App.5th at 303-304.) Defendant's reliance on Civil Code Sec. 2923.6 does not change the analysis. First, it is unclear whether the 30-day appeal notice was really a step antecedent to enforcing a security interest. Second, the conflicting language in the letters about collection clearly implicate the Act. Lastly, Civil Code Sec. 2924(b) shows that only the trustee on the deed of trust (and not a mortgage servicer such as Defendant) is immune from liability under the Rosenthal Act. Plaintiffs sufficiently pleads damages. Paragraph 28 alleges emotional distress damages, which are recoverable under the Rosenthal Act. (Civ. Code Sec. 1788.30(b) [debtor may recover actual damages for willful and knowing violation]; Komarova v. National Credit Acceptance, Inc. (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 324, 341 n.3 ["It is not disputed . . . that 'actual damages' recoverable under the Act (Sec. 1788.30) include those for the plaintiff's emotional distress."]. Plaintiffs can also recover a penalty under Civil Code Sec. 1788.30(b). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PORTFOLIO RECOVERY ASSOCIATES LLC VS. DAVID FARBE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion calendar for Thursday, April 25, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT DAVID FARBE'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Complaint. Nonparty David Farber's motion to quash service of summons and complaint is denied. Although the complaint identifies the name of the defendant as David Farbe, a slight misspelling does not render service defective if the person served is aware that he is the person named as a defendant. (Sakaguchi v. Sakaguchi (2009) 173 Cal.App.4th 852, 857.) Here, the proof of service shows that Mr. Farber was personally served with the summons and complaint and confirmed his identity to the process server. Mr. Farber's contention that he was the victim of identity theft and is not responsible for the debt is not a proper subject of a motion to quash, and may be raised on a dispositive motion. Both parties are directed to the provisions of the Identity Theft Resolution Act concerning a debt collector's responsibilities upon receiving specified information from a debtor that he or she is a victim of identity theft. (See Civ. Code Sec. 1788.18.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case nameand case number. Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
